cards.title: Write WorkBoard vs claude-mem comparison — knowledge-graph framing
cards.origin: can u do a brief comparison of claude-mem and this? how this may be better than claude mem? cos it doesnt store memory and this allows to traverse the worflow like a knowledge graph? maybe include this 'study' somewhere? idk? what do u think? is it more efficient than claude-mem? cos wb can traverse the 'jist of things' such as reading subtasks or titles --> subtasks or 'why it happened' etc.
cards.writeup:
Wrote an honest WorkBoard vs claude-mem comparison built around the user's own framing: 'knowledge graph of work, not a memory store.'

USER QUESTION (the seed)
'can u do a brief comparison of claude-mem and this? how this may be better than claude mem? cos it doesnt store memory and this allows to traverse the worflow like a knowledge graph? wb can traverse the jist of things such as reading subtasks or titles --> subtasks or why it happened.'

DELIVERED
- docs/COMPARISON.md (NEW, 98 lines) — the long form:
  • Side-by-side shape comparison (memory store vs knowledge graph).
  • A graph-walk example proving the 'jist traversal' point: list query → card → subtasks → writeup → links.
  • Honest 'Where claude-mem is genuinely better' section (semantic recall of fuzzy memories, cross-project scope, no carding discipline required, backfilling untracked past).
  • Decision matrix: 7 representative questions and which tool to pick.
  • Caveats section: 'this is written by the WorkBoard project, please open an issue if we misrepresent.'
- README §🤔 rewritten — now leads with the shape difference, shows the graph-walk example, links to COMPARISON.md for tradeoffs. No marketing fluff, owns the limitations.
- docs/README.md index updated to list COMPARISON.md.

KEY FRAMING
'claude-mem stores memory. WorkBoard is a knowledge graph of work.' Validates user's intuition that the structured-traversal model is qualitatively different from embedding-similarity recall, not just 'better' on some axis. Both can coexist; they solve different halves.

COMMIT
- cdecfb8 'docs: honest WorkBoard vs claude-mem comparison (knowledge-graph framing)'
- Pushed origin/main (5e0bcc2..cdecfb8).

cards.title: Extend demo_lifecycle.sh — add bug-cycle story arc (Done→IP-w/bug→Done loop)
cards.origin: actually, can u after 4/4 do --> Done --> IP with Bug then make 4/5 then 5/5 --> Done --> then IP back with a bug --> Done. Change the script u wrote.
cards.writeup:
Extended dev/demo_lifecycle.sh with the 3-arc bug-cycle story.

NEW STORY ARC (one 'do #N')
- Arc 1: Task → IP → tick original subtasks → Done (v1: happy path).
- Arc 2: Done → IP w/ bug via 'card.py bug --reason DEMO:...' (auto-adds 5th subtask, now N/N+1) → tick it (N+1/N+1) → Done (v2: bug fixed).
- Arc 3: Done → IP w/ bug (no --reason, no new subtask) → Done (v3: cosmetic fix).

KEY FIX (caught during smoke test)
'card.py bug --reason X' adds a subtask with text '🐞 fix bug: X' — NOT 'X'. My initial 'DEMO:' prefix detection (case-statement + awk regex) never matched because the actual prefix is the bug emoji, not 'DEMO:'. Switched to a single python helper q() that supports modes 'sids' / 'sids_bug' / 'last_bug' / 'count' — pure-python, no awk/case quirks with multibyte chars.

RESET HARDENED
- Removes any 🐞-prefixed subtask (the bug-added ones — minted by card.py bug).
- Unchecks the remaining originals.
- Clears writeup, strips bug tag. Idempotent.

SMOKE TESTED
- Reset → run 0.2 → end state: Done, 6/6, tags=[] (bug tag cleared by the final fly to Done). 
- Reset again → Task, 0/4, tags=[]. Clean.
- Real-paced run (1.5s) just executed at user trigger (rev 1286 → 1296), all 3 arcs visible.

NEXT-USE TEMPLATE
'do #N' → dev/demo_lifecycle.sh N run     # 3-arc story, ~16s wall-clock at default pace.

cards.title: Write reusable dev/demo_lifecycle.sh — instant card-lifecycle screen-record
cards.origin: u thought for too long, can u write a script or function that u can just run? because u 'thinking with high effort then there is an awkward pause then i said do #669 then u were just thinking.
cards.writeup:
Wrote dev/demo_lifecycle.sh — parameterized lifecycle demo, executable, bash-3.2-compatible.

USAGE
  dev/demo_lifecycle.sh <num> reset             # park card in Task / 0/N / no writeup
  dev/demo_lifecycle.sh <num> run [pace_sec]    # Task→IP→tick each subtask→Done (default 1.5s)

WHY
User: 'u thought for too long, can u write a script or function that u can just run?' — every demo previously needed me to reason through reset+pacing+sids inline. Now any 'do #N' is one line, zero thinking.

KEY DETAILS
- Picks up subtask sids dynamically via 'card.py show' + python3 → works for ANY card with subtasks (not hard-coded to #669).
- Bash 3.2 compatible (macOS default): originally used 'mapfile' which is bash 4+ → fixed with a portable while-read loop.
- chmod +x set.
- Lives in dev/ (gitignored) — won't ship to the public plugin.

SMOKE TESTED
- dev/demo_lifecycle.sh 669 reset → '✓ #669 reset → Task, 0/4, writeup cleared'
- dev/demo_lifecycle.sh 669 run 0.3 → full 6-step lifecycle (rev 1210 → 1216), landed Done 4/4 with writeup.
- Reset again → parked at 0/4 ready for the next recording.

NEXT-USE TEMPLATE
When user says 'do #N' → I run: dev/demo_lifecycle.sh N run     (single Bash call, instant).

cards.title: Clear stray multi-element text selection on background click
cards.origin: when i 'highlight' the columns, or multiple columns, i also want to click an empty area and make it go away
cards.writeup:
Extended the #659 deselect handler to also clear stray text-selections that span MULTIPLE column titles / cards. (Originally #51 on the fresh board; re-added here.)

USER REPORT
"when i 'highlight' the columns, or multiple columns, i also want to click an empty area and make it go away, just now was 'within the text box'"

ROOT CAUSE
#659's handler bailed when activeElement.isContentEditable === false. Drag-selecting across multiple sibling column headers leaves NO single contentEditable focused → handler exited and blue stuck — same #555-class root: drag handlers preventDefault the mousedown, suppressing browser-default click-clears-selection.

CHANGE (templates/board.html ~3516)
Same capture-phase mousedown listener, now two branches:
- (a) #659 path — single focused contentEditable: blur (commits the rename) + clear, as before.
- (b) #51 path — no focused editable: if a non-collapsed Range exists AND click is OUTSIDE any contenteditable/input/textarea AND OUTSIDE the selection's own range (intersectsNode), clear it. Re-drag-inside and click-into-edit preserved.

COMMIT
- 6b580f8 "fix(board): clear stray multi-element text selection on background click (#51)"
- Pushed origin/main.

cards.title: Backend after_fill signal to fully close #385 residual (abnormal reconcile bail)
cards.origin: Follow-up to #385. The client fix (reset _bootstrapFillSeen on every final) eliminates all normal-termination sticks, but a bootstrap reconcile that ABNORMALLY bails emitting ZERO final events can still leave the flag set → the NEXT recon's final misfires flipResort once (then self-corrects). Complete fix = backend emits an explicit after_fill/bootstrap=true field on the reconcile event so the client drops the sticky flag entirely. Touches: serve.py /progress whitelist (add field), hourly_reconcile _emit_progress, bootstrap-vs-live caller plumbing, board.html (use d.bootstrap, delete _bootstrapFillSeen). Low priority — cosmetic, abnormal-termination only.

cards.title: Column-title selection stays blue until you open/close a card
cards.origin: when i highlight the Column Title eg: 'Done' ... it turns blue. then when i click elsewhere, the title is still blue. i need to click open a card then close. i want such that if i click a background or smth, it goes back to normal
cards.writeup:
Fixed: column-title text-selection ("blue") no longer sticks until you open/close a card — clicking the background now resets it.

ROOT CAUSE
Column titles are contentEditable (templates/board.html ~5672, nameEl). Selecting one paints the browser ::selection (blue) and focuses it (.name:focus{background:#fff}). Clicking elsewhere should collapse the selection, but the card/column mousedown handlers preventDefault (same root cause as #555's Ctrl+A note), so a background click never reached the browser's deselect — the only thing that cleared it was the focus churn of opening+closing a card.

FIX (templates/board.html, right after the #555 Ctrl+A handler ~line 3520)
Added a capture-phase document 'mousedown' listener: if a contentEditable is currently focused AND the mousedown target is OUTSIDE it, blur() it (its own blur handler commits the rename) and window.getSelection().removeAllRanges() to clear the stray selection. Capture phase so it runs before the drag handlers swallow the event. Scoped to fire ONLY when an editable is focused, so normal text-selection/drag everywhere else is untouched.

VERIFICATION (code logic — Playwright MCP was env-broken this session: "URL.canParse is not a function"; user said ignore it)
- Reported flow: focus title → blue selection → click bg → handler: activeElement is the title (isContentEditable), target not contained → blur (white bg reverts) + removeAllRanges (blue clears) → normal. ✓
- Edge cases hold: double-click/drag-select inside the title (a.contains(target) → skip, preserved); first click to focus (activeElement=body → early return); click to another editable (old commits, new focuses); rest of board untouched (only acts when an editable is focused). ✓
- node --check on the inserted snippet: parses cleanly. Confirmed present in served HTML at :7891 (served directly from templates/board.html, no rebuild). Reload to pick up.
- NOT committed/pushed (pre-release gate).

SAFETY CHECK DONE FIRST

- git check-ignore dev/smoke_test.py → ignored.

DECISIONS

FOLLOW-UP (minor, separate): docs/DEVELOPMENT.md, docs/DISCOVERY.md, docs/BOOTSTRAP.md still reference dev/ scripts that won't be in the public clone. Stale contributor instructions, not runtime breakage. Update or label as maintainer-local if/when doing a docs polish pass.

cards.title: Sort the board at end of declutter BEFORE the ✓ HUD completes (settle 🧹 sweep header)
cards.origin: user: 🧹 sweep header ends at bottom during one-by-one declutter, only correct on refresh; SortByTime at END of declutter, final_hud shows after sorting
cards.writeup: Shipped 0.9.25. Commit 9ffbd7d (templates/board.html). Originally #157 on the lossy re-mine; re-applied on canonical. Linked to #655. The 🧹 sweep header sinks during one-by-one declutter (cards glide in on top); only refresh fixed it. #156 moved the final emit to after declutter, so flipResort now runs at the true end. #157: for the bootstrap end (reconcile+declutter), flipResort FIRST so the header settles above its cards, THEN show ✓ COMPLETE after the sort glide (750ms) — final_hud is the genuine last step. SSE ordering guarantees declutter card-moves are in the DOM. node --check clean.

cards.title: Bootstrap HUD stays open until declutter finishes; final count tallies reconcile + declutter
cards.origin: user: HUD disappears and Declutter is still running; want ONLY after declutter is done then show N cards brought up to date (reconcile+declutter)
cards.writeup: Shipped 0.9.25. Commit 20c7c29 (hourly_reconcile.py + hourly_extractor.py). Originally #156 on the lossy re-mine; re-applied on canonical. reconcile_sweep emitted final=True -> JS HUD done()+auto-hide BEFORE declutter_sweep ran, so the HUD vanished mid-declutter and the count was reconcile-only. Fix: reconcile_sweep gains final_hud (bootstrap passes False -> non-final 'tidying up' line keeps HUD open); declutter emits a 'tidying N' line; run() emits ONE combined final '✓ (reconcile+declutter) brought up to date' after declutter, guarded by not-failed_buckets. Unit-tested.

cards.title: Declutter sweep glides at 45ms/card (was ~650ms)
cards.origin: user: declutter i want 45ms
cards.writeup: Shipped 0.9.25. Commit 889b15d (hourly_reconcile.py). Originally carded as #155 on a transient lossy re-mine; re-applied here on the canonical board. Declutter glides at 45ms/card (was ~650ms = 400ms inherited --pause-ms default + 250ms loop dwell). Added _DECLUTTER_PACE_MS=45 passed as --pause-ms on the single fly call; removed the time.sleep(0.25) loop dwell. Deliberate per-feature pace (not a per-session sim override). Applies on next bootstrap declutter.

cards.title: Card flown into collapsed Done lands at bottom (outside its date group) + target day doesn't expand
cards.origin: user: when i CLOSE all and move a card (or agent moves IP→Done today), it goes to the LAST/bottom instead of the correct date, and the date should expand
cards.writeup:
Commit (board.html). Follow-up to #652; reproduced from the user's screenshot (#532 flown to Done landed at the very bottom, outside TODAY's group, while all days collapsed).

ROOT CAUSE: Done is date-grouped, but the two INCREMENTAL move paths bypass grouping. (1) simulateUserDragMove (agent/CLI/SSE ghost-flight + kebab/select moves) inserts the new card before the first .card in the destination — with all days collapsed there are NO .card elements, so insertBefore(.., null) appends at the bottom, and the day never opens. (2) commitCardDrag (user drag) deliberately skips render() ('DOM already correct') so a drop into a collapsed day stays mis-placed.

FIX (both, scoped to Done): auto-expand the card's target day, and when its slot isn't currently rendered (folded, or no anchor card) do a full grouped render() so the card lands under its date header. Normal 'ship into open today' keeps the ghost-flight glide. commitCardDrag renders only when the dropped day was collapsed. Still render-time only — never mutates state.cards/doneAt/order, so flipResort sort and the fly glide are untouched.

VERIFIED: node --check clean; traced agent-move (collapsed today → render → lands under TODAY) / user-drag-into-collapsed / open-today-glide-preserved / new-day-no-anchor → render. Live on next board reload. NOT pushed yet.

cards.title: Collapsible Done-by-day groups + collapse-all dated index (wayfinding)
cards.origin: user: make the Done date headers collapsable; at a glance see where day 5 of 10 is / where to scroll. Must NOT interfere with flying cards or sorting (fragile). Approved: Collapse + index (recommended).
cards.writeup:
Commit f28661e (templates/board.html). Approved scope: Collapse + index (recommended).

WHAT: (1) click a Done date header to fold/unfold that day (chevron ▸/▾); (2) a ⊟/⊞ toggle in the Done column header collapses/expands ALL days → Done becomes a compact dated index (date + count per row) for at-a-glance wayfinding; (3) clicking a collapsed day expands it AND scrolls it into view (subtask 4); (4) a card landing in a collapsed day auto-expands it (subtask 3).

HOW (robust, non-fragile — the user's explicit concern): collapse is a RENDER-TIME FILTER, never DOM surgery / never data mutation. _collapsedDays (Set of ymd) persisted to localStorage; the Done render loop just skips a collapsed day's cards (header+count still render). It never touches state.cards / doneAt / ordering → flipResort 'Sort Time' and card-fly are completely unaffected. Auto-expand reuses the existing _flashIds so a freshly-shipped card in a folded day never vanishes. Done is already day-grouped + LIFO-sorted each render, so there's no manual Done reorder for collapse to clobber. Shared _doneGroupTs/_doneDays helpers; chevron + role=button/aria-expanded for a11y.

VERIFIED: node --check clean; node logic tests — _doneDays grouping (incl. discovered→createdAt), collapse-all/expand-all, auto-expand-on-flash all pass. Code-reviewed interference points (fly/sort/drag/SSE) — none mutate or are mutated by collapse. Applies live on next board reload. NOT pushed/version-bumped yet.

subtasks.text: Render-time per-day collapse filter (persisted Set) + chevron toggle on day-header click
Collapse-all / expand-all toggle in the Done column header
Auto-expand the target day when a card lands in a collapsed day (fly/drag into Done)
Click a collapsed day → expand + scroll into view

cards.title: Redesign the 🧹 First-run sweep divider (ui-ux-pro-max): SVG icon, real tokens, structured parts
cards.origin: user: use UI UX promax — '🧹 First-run sweep · 2026-06-16 · 14 items' its kinda ugly
cards.writeup:
Commit ed4562b (templates/board.html). Used the ui-ux-pro-max skill.

PROBLEM: the section-header was one flat string "🧹 First-run sweep · 2026-06-16 · 14 items" colored via an UNDEFINED CSS var --fg (fell back to #cfd6df), which is near-invisible on the light pastel Discarded column — washed out / ugly.

FIX: structured, quiet divider built in renderCardCompact:
- inline SVG broom (NOT emoji — ui-ux-pro-max §no-emoji-icons), tinted --accent.
- label "First-run sweep" on the REAL warm token --muted (hover → --ink).
- count "14" in a tabular-figure pill (bg --neutral).
- a hairline gradient rule (--ink 14%) that extends to group the swept cards below.
- right-aligned short date "Jun 16" on --muted.
Parses the canonical title with a raw-title fallback; role=button + aria-label(full text) for a11y.

VERIFIED: rendered a faithful before/after via headless Chrome (real boho tokens + pastel column) — old was illegibly faint, new is clean+legible; node --check passes. Screenshot shared with user.

cards.title: Discarded cards (incl. 🧹 sweep header) vanish on hard refresh — Discarded column not materialized on load
cards.origin: user: why when i hard refresh the sweep icon is gone? it should stay there until the user clears it
cards.writeup:
Commit ed4562b (templates/board.html).

ROOT CAUSE: the Discarded column is NOT persisted in board.json — it's lazily created only on the first soft-delete (discardColumn(), called solely from discardCard()/emptyDiscarded()). The bootstrap declutter adds cards with column:'discarded' SERVER-SIDE, never creating the column. So on a hard refresh, _absorbState() loaded a board with no Discarded column and every discarded card (incl. the 🧹 sweep header) had no column to render into → silently vanished until a manual soft-delete recreated it. (Confirmed: #649's data persisted in board.json across loads — pure render-materialization bug, not data loss.)

FIX: ensureDiscardColumnIfNeeded() materializes the Discarded column (stacked under super-urgent to match SPEC) whenever any card has column:'discarded'. Called from _absorbState() (every load + SSE state-replace — the guaranteed choke point, since enterApp doesn't call render()) AND render(). Idempotent; client-side only (next scheduleSave persists it; a fresh load re-materializes regardless).

VERIFIED: node unit test — create / idempotent / no-discarded-noop / existing-by-name-no-dup / no-parent-stackUnder-null all pass; node --check clean.

cards.title: Bootstrap HUD: remove 'still working… Ns' tick + cut inter-phase latency (redundant harvest re-parse)
cards.origin: user: remove the 'still working.. Ns' tick (keep the 1/10 count); gaps before first chunk + before reconciliation take long — bug or design?
cards.notes: Reverts the #638 heartbeat tick + dedupes the bootstrap harvest. (Originally carded as #121 on a transient re-mined board; re-applied here to the canonical board.) Shipped in 0.9.24.
cards.writeup:
Shipped in 0.9.24 (pushed to origin/main, HEAD f5b3258). Two atomic commits:

(1) 0587b56 — removed the 'still working… Ns' HUD tick (reverts #638). progress_heartbeat() in hourly_emit.py is now a no-op context manager (no daemon thread, no emit); _HudPulse.touch() inert; dropped unused threading import. The N/M chunk count + 'N card(s) emitted so far' sub-line are UNTOUCHED. grep 'still working' scripts/*.py → 0.

(2) 268d383 — per-bootstrap harvest cache in hourly_extractor.py. @_bootstrap_harvest_cache scopes a memo to run() (cleared in finally → the long-lived server never serves stale events to a later SessionStart recon). _flatten_events reuses an exact (project,days,sources) parse instead of re-reading every transcript. No cross-window filtering → each window's dedup result byte-identical. Collapses the tier-2==reconcile duplicate parse.

ANSWER to 'bug or design?': the 60-90s reconcile wait is mostly the inherent Haiku reconcile call (by design); the first-chunk gap is inherent parsing + LLM cold-start. But ~7s of the pre-reconcile gap was a genuine redundancy (reconcile re-parsed the identical window tier-2 had just read) — that slice is now gone. NOT a server-health issue (CPU ~0%).

MEASURED (live transcripts, 8-day window): run()'s harvest pattern 13.24s → 6.25s (~7s saved); identical 12954-event result set. All 37 modules import clean. Manifests bumped 0.9.23→0.9.24 (f5b3258).

Note: this work was first carded as #121 on a transient re-mined board (lossy bootstrap); re-applied here on the restored canonical board as #648.

subtasks.text: Replace/remove the 'still working… Ns' tick (no-op heartbeat)
Memoize _flatten_events so transcripts parse once per bootstrap

cards.title: Cap final-bucket extraction time so it can't grind ~80s (most liekly not neede)
cards.origin: Split from #638 subtask 4: the last/biggest bucket (active session) can grind through Haiku + recursive sub-bucket retries for ~80s. #638 now keeps the HUD alive during it (heartbeat), but a hard time-cap would also bound the wall-clock. Riskier (could truncate work) and ties into #627 silent-drop handling — do as its own card.
cards.writeup:
FUTURE IMPROVEMENT — not currently needed (assessed 2026-06-16). Was split from #638 subtask 4; reclassified from bug to idea.

IDEA: put a hard wall-clock cap on extracting the final/biggest bucket (the active session), which can grind ~80s through Haiku + the recursive sub-bucket retry ladder.

WHY IT'S NOT NEEDED: #638 already fixed the actual user-facing problem — the HUD no longer LOOKS frozen during the grind (it shows a live 'still working… mm:ss' heartbeat), and #627/#640/#642/#645 guarantee the slow bucket is never silently dropped (failure is recorded, retried in-fill, and auto-backfilled next session). So the ~80s is now VISIBLE and SURVIVABLE — legitimate work being extracted, not a hang.

WHY A HARD CAP IS RISKY: cutting a bucket off mid-extraction would TRUNCATE genuine work → drop its cards, re-introducing the exact silent-drop class #640 closed (unless the cap routes the timed-out bucket to failed_buckets → but then #645 just re-attempts the same slow bucket next session, deferring not solving). Trading completeness for speed on a ONE-TIME-per-board bootstrap is a poor bargain.

SAFER LEVERS IF SPEED EVER MATTERS (prefer these over a truncating cap): more --workers, larger --chunk-size (amortize claude -p cold-start), or a smaller --discover-days window. All already tunable.

REVISIT-TRIGGER: build only if real users complain bootstrap is too SLOW (not merely that it looked frozen — that's fixed) AND the worker/chunk/window levers prove insufficient. Even then, prefer a cap that routes to #645 backfill over one that drops. Related: #638 (HUD heartbeat), #628 (inline fallback idea).

cards.title: Split hourly_extractor.py (1186 LOC) — extract LLM dispatch + retry ladder
cards.origin: VISION RULE 1 bounded-files (~1000 LOC ceiling): hourly_extractor.py was 1066 before #640, now 1186. Pre-existing smell worsened by #627 work. Split the cohesive LLM-dispatch concern (extract_cards_for_chunk / _extract_chunk_with_retries / ChunkExtractionError / extract_cards_for_hour) into a focused leaf (e.g. hourly_extract_llm.py) per the #307 pattern; keep the public interface stable; update the VISION arch map in the same commit.
cards.writeup: Extracted the LLM-dispatch + retry-ladder cluster into a new leaf scripts/hourly_extract_llm.py (184 LOC): ChunkExtractionError, extract_cards_for_chunk, extract_cards_for_hour, _extract_chunk_with_retries. Acyclic (→hourly_common only); public interface stable via re-export from hourly_extractor. hourly_extractor.py 1219→1065 LOC (~pre-#640 baseline). Test seam moved to leaf namespace in dev/test_627_silent_drop.py. VISION.md arch map updated same commit. Verified: test_627 17/17, smoke 14/14, /e2e 48/0, live board untouched. Commit db9eedd (pushed).

cards.title: Auto-backfill recorded failed_buckets on next SessionStart (#627 follow-up)
cards.origin: follow-up to #640: replay-state now records partial:true + failed_buckets; a later pass could auto re-extract them instead of needing a manual re-run.
cards.writeup: Auto-backfill of bootstrap-dropped buckets on SessionStart — the payoff of #640. _mark_replay_complete now records bucket_min; new _backfill_failed_buckets(project, board) re-harvests the window covering the oldest recorded failure, re-bucketizes at the recorded bucket_min, retries each via _extract_chunk_with_retries, emits recovered cards, and rewrites the gate (recovered+aged-out cleared, still-failing kept; converges by dropping un-backfillable buckets). Serialized via _boardio.recon_lock; runs before reconcile in --reconcile-only so recovered cards get reconciled. Architecture: addition to hourly_extractor (owns SessionStart recon), downward-only _boardio import, no cycle; serves the VISION reconciliation star. VISION module-count invariant corrected 34→37. Verified: test_645_backfill.py 15 checks, test_627 17/17, smoke 14/14, /e2e 48/0, live board untouched. Commit ef483e2 (pushed).

cards.title: Test gaps: #384 gate-reopen-on-exception + recon_lock contention
cards.origin: review F5: no test fires an exception in reconcile_sweep to prove the gate reopens; no recon_lock contention stress; _SEC_PER_CHUNK estimate model unvalidated.
cards.writeup: Filled the three review-F5 test gaps (test-only, no prod change): E1 #384 gate-reopen-on-exception (drives real run() with a raising reconcile_sweep, asserts the try/finally still flips completed_card_replay=1 so recon can't stick); E2 recon_lock contention (held → second acquire False non-blocking, reacquirable after release); E3 estimate_fill model (buckets/chunks counts + eta_sec=ceil(chunks/workers)*_SEC_PER_CHUNK across workers 1/2/8). dev/test_644_gate_locks_estimate.py 19 checks. Full suite green: smoke 14/14, test_627/645/641/643 pass. Commit f93dc43 (pushed).

cards.title: No CAS on direct (no-server) board writes → lost update
cards.origin: review F4: server POST path has rev-CAS (#609) but card.py direct-write fallback relies on board_lock alone (5s timeout → proceeds unlocked); two timed-out writers can lose an update.
cards.writeup: Gave the direct (no-server) write path the rev-CAS the server POST path already had (#609). Gap: main()'s server branch loads UNLOCKED; if the server vanishes mid-command, atomic_save's self-locked direct write bumps base_rev+1 from an unlocked read → clobbers a writer that bumped rev in the gap. Fix: _assert_base_rev re-reads on-disk rev under the lock, raises BoardConflict on mismatch (server-branch retry loop reloads+retries); applied to both direct branches; _current_rev→None skips check so first write never false-conflicts; no-server branch catches BoardConflict → clean exit. Verified: test_643_direct_cas.py 8 checks, smoke 14/14 (card.py no-server lifecycle 12/12), test_627/645/641 green, /e2e 48/0, live board untouched. Commit ce03382 (pushed).

cards.title: Replay gate-file write OSError swallowed (start/complete)
cards.origin: review F3: _mark_replay_started/_mark_replay_complete swallow OSError. Start-write fail → gate never blocks recon; complete-write fail → gate stuck closed → all future SessionStart recon stand down forever.
cards.writeup: Commit c5321cf pushed to origin/main. Root cause: _mark_replay_complete swallowed OSError → completed_card_replay stayed 0 on disk → SessionStart recon permanently stood down for that board. Fix (hourly_extractor.py gate helpers): added _write_replay_state atomic write (tempfile+os.replace); _mark_replay_complete now FAILS OPEN (unlinks stale state so _replay_complete defaults True) on write failure; _mark_replay_started fails to default-open. Verified: test_627_silent_drop.py L4 (4 new checks); smoke 14/14; /e2e recon 8/0, replay-gate intact, live board untouched.

cards.title: Recon gate TOCTOU: _replay_complete checked before recon_lock
cards.origin: review F2: _run_reconcile_only reads gate without a lock before reconcile_sweep takes the non-blocking recon_lock; gate can flip in the gap. Low severity (recon_lock non-blocking skip self-mitigates).
cards.writeup: Closed the recon-gate TOCTOU: _run_reconcile_only checked _replay_complete before reconcile_sweep took recon_lock, so a bootstrap fill starting in the gap could be raced. Fix: reconcile_sweep grows an optional gate callable re-evaluated under recon_lock (atomic check+act); SessionStart passes gate=lambda: _replay_complete(board), cheap pre-check kept as early-out. Plain callable keeps dependency one-directional (no hourly_reconcile→hourly_extractor cycle). Also closed same TOCTOU in _backfill_failed_buckets (#645). Verified: test_641_gate_toctou.py 6 checks, test_627 17/17, test_645 15/15, smoke 14/14, /e2e 48/0, live board untouched. Commit e3b668f (pushed).

cards.title: Tier-2 silent drop: failed bucket marked replay-complete (#627)
cards.origin: user: review bootstrap 12/13→recon for racing/silent-drop; fix #627 — tier-2 bugged out at bucket 12/13 but marked replay complete anyway, dropping cards
cards.writeup: Fixed #627 silent tier-2 drop. Root cause: extract_cards_for_chunk returned [] on the 3 genuine-failure paths (subprocess err / non-zero exit / non-JSON) identically to a legit empty-digest bucket, so a failed bucket was indistinguishable from 'no work' and run() called _mark_replay_complete regardless. Fix (scripts/hourly_extractor.py): (1) ChunkExtractionError raised on failure, [] only for empty; (2) retry ladder drives off the exception and re-raises when exhausted unrecovered; (3) _extract_haiku records failed_buckets + a bounded recovery retry pass + returns (n_cards, failed_buckets); (4) _run_window threads the tuple; (5) run() tier-fly accumulates failures across tiers, stamps replay-state partial:true + failed_buckets (gate still reopens — #384 preserved), loud stderr + degraded final HUD instead of clean ✓ COMPLETE. Verified: dev/test_627_silent_drop.py (13 checks: failure≠empty, perma-fail recorded not dropped, transient-fail recovered, gate partial-stamp+reopen) PASS; smoke_test 14/14; /e2e 48/0 incl. recon_replay_gate, live board untouched. NOT yet committed.

cards.title: HUD card-count resets to 0 at tier-1→tier-2 boundary
cards.origin: user: during bootstrap, 'N cards emitted so far' restarts from 0 when it 'speeds up' instead of continuing from the replay total
cards.writeup: Commit b618877 (pushed to origin/main). Thread cumulative cards_offset through tier-fly in scripts/hourly_extractor.py: _extract_haiku takes offset + returns window count, _run_window forwards/returns it, run() passes tier-1 count as tier-2 offset. HUD now shows cards_offset+n_cards. Syntax-checked; matches test_hud_single.py:168 expectation.

cards.title: Bootstrap HUD freezes ~1-2min at 'N-1/N' (looks hung) — no sub-progress within a slow chunk or during reconcile
cards.origin: June-10 video: HUD hit 12/13 then sat in 'stasis' ~1min20s, then board flipped to the full board. want to make sure this doesnt happen to users
cards.notes: EVIDENCE: screen recording (2026-06-10 20:46) shows bootstrap HUD freeze at 12/13 for ~80s, then board re-renders to final state. ROOT CAUSE (code-confirmed, NOT just an SSE race): the HUD only ticks once per COMPLETED chunk (hourly_extractor _extract_haiku) — NO within-chunk sub-progress — AND the end-of-replay reconcile (hourly_extractor.py:922 reconcile_sweep, no banner/HUD driver) emits NO progress (grep: nobody emits phase='reconcile'). So any slow stage freezes the HUD at its last value. The 80s freeze = the LAST/biggest bucket (active session) grinding through Haiku + recursive-subbucket retries (and/or the reconcile sweep) with zero feedback. DETERMINISTIC, not a fluke — recurs whenever the recent bucket is large (it usually is). The dropped final progress event (12/13 vs 13/13) IS a best-effort SSE race (_emit_progress 4s + cmd_progress 2s timeouts, swallowed) but is secondary. TIE-IN: that frozen 13th bucket was the SAME one that failed → dropped #582-625 (see #627). RISK: not data loss itself (board renders after) BUT looks hung → user may force-quit/reload mid-bootstrap. FIX: (1) within-chunk heartbeat / indeterminate spinner so a slow chunk isn't a frozen number; (2) drive the HUD during reconcile (wire phase='reconcile' progress — header already exists); (3) on a stuck/retrying chunk show 'still working… retry N'; (4) cap final-bucket extraction time so it can't grind 80s. Related: #627 (silent bucket drop).
cards.writeup: HUD heartbeat so bootstrap progress never freezes on a stale number during a slow/silent stage (the ~80s last-bucket grind + the 60-90s end-of-replay reconcile). New leaf hourly_emit.progress_heartbeat + _HudPulse: daemon thread emits 'still working… mm:ss' only when a stage is quiet > interval (reads live done/total/label; callers .touch() so it fires only on real stalls; SSE-only, no lock contention). Wired into _extract_haiku (as_completed loop + #627 recovery pass) and reconcile_sweep (_llm_reconcile call). Subtasks 1-3 done; subtask 4 (hard time-cap) split to #647. Verified: test_638_hud_heartbeat.py 10 checks, smoke 14/14 (name-audit incl.), /e2e 48/0, live board untouched. Commit fa80ac0 (pushed).

subtasks.text: HUD heartbeat mechanism: pulse 'still working… mm:ss' when a stage stalls > interval (leaf in hourly_emit)
Wire heartbeat into _extract_haiku (as_completed loop + recovery/retry pass) — within-chunk liveness
Drive HUD during the end-of-replay reconcile sweep (the 60-90s silent Haiku call)

cards.title: TRADEOFF: _file_lock proceeds UNLOCKED on >1s timeout (best-effort, never-block)
cards.origin: #633 code-review finding 6 (PLAUSIBLE). Accepted by user 2026-06-16.
cards.notes:
WHERE: scripts/port_registry.py → _file_lock(); used by set_active, assign,
set_port, and _hook_stop_recon._write_recon_pending.

BEHAVIOR: the cross-process flock has a 1.0s timeout. On timeout it yields False
and the caller proceeds with the read-modify-write ANYWAY (unlocked). Under
sustained >1s contention on the lock file, the assign() TOCTOU and set_active
clobber that the lock exists to prevent could momentarily re-appear, silently.

WHY ACCEPTED: this is the deliberate "a Stop hook / card write must NEVER block or
raise" contract. The locked critical section is a sub-millisecond write to a tiny
JSON file, so 1s of contention means ~hundreds of processes hammering the same
lock simultaneously — not a real single-human, few-boards scenario. Blocking
indefinitely or raising would be a WORSE failure (a hung/aborted card write). The
degradation is bounded and improbable; correctness holds for all realistic load
(verified: 50 parallel assigns → 50 distinct ports, stable across reruns).

ALTERNATIVE IF WE REVISIT: raise the timeout, or escalate to a retry-with-backoff
on the yielded False, or (for assign only) fail loudly like card.py's direct-write
path does on lock-timeout.

REVISIT IF: profiling ever shows real lock-timeout events in normal use. Until
then: INTENTIONAL best-effort by design. Ref commit 0406d2b.

cards.title: TRADEOFF: _prune_sessions 32-cap can evict a long-idle but still-live session
cards.origin: #633 code-review finding 5 (PLAUSIBLE). Accepted by user 2026-06-16.
cards.notes:
WHERE: scripts/port_registry.py → _prune_sessions(sessions, keep=32).

BEHAVIOR: the last-active sessions map is capped at 32 entries, kept by newest
`at`. A session that goes quiet (stops touching cards) but is still OPEN, with its
board still on disk, can be evicted if 32 OTHER sessions write after it. Its tier-1
routing entry is then lost and it falls back to global. The docstring says "never
evict by age" — the size cap is an *implicit* age bias for sessions that go silent.

WHY ACCEPTED: triggering this needs 32+ DISTINCT concurrent Claude sessions on one
machine for a single human — far beyond realistic. And the failure is graceful:
the evicted session falls back to `global`, not to a crash. 32 is already generous
headroom over any real multi-board workflow.

ALTERNATIVE IF WE REVISIT: raise keep to 64/128 (cheap), or prune only by board-
gone-from-disk (drop the size cap entirely and rely on dead-board GC).

REVISIT IF: real usage ever approaches dozens of simultaneous sessions, or a user
reports wrong-board routing after heavy parallel session churn. Until then:
INTENTIONAL. Ref commit 0406d2b.

cards.title: TRADEOFF: get_active() falls back to global when a session's own board was deleted
cards.origin: #633 code-review finding 4 (PLAUSIBLE). Accepted by user 2026-06-16 (user chose to keep the resilient fallback, NOT return None).
cards.notes:
WHERE: scripts/port_registry.py → get_active(session_id) tiered self-heal.

BEHAVIOR: tier-1 = this session's own board (if it still exists on disk); if that
board dir was DELETED/moved, it falls through to tier-2 = global.board, which may
be a DIFFERENT live session's board. So a session whose board vanished mid-life,
running card.py from $HOME, could resolve another session's board instead of
getting a clean "no board" error.

WHY ACCEPTED (user decision 2026-06-16): the fallback only triggers from $HOME
with no cwd board AND after the session's own board was deleted — a rare combo.
The global fallback is the SAME mechanism a fresh/unseen session uses correctly,
and it keeps the tool resilient (a moved board still self-heals) rather than dead-
ending the user with an error. We considered returning None (forcing --board) for
the specific "own entry existed but board gone" case; user opted to KEEP the
resilient global fallback.

ALTERNATIVE IF WE REVISIT: in get_active, if session_id had an entry whose board
is now gone, return None instead of falling to global — trades resilience for a
louder, safer "pass --board" error.

REVISIT IF: a user reports a card landing on the wrong project's board after
deleting/moving a board mid-session. Until then: INTENTIONAL. Ref commit 0406d2b.

cards.title: TRADEOFF: recon_pending merge keeps only the last writer's `activity`
cards.origin: #633 code-review finding 3 (CONFIRMED). Accepted by user 2026-06-16.
cards.notes:
WHERE: scripts/_hook_stop_recon.py → _write_recon_pending() (the merge branch).

BEHAVIOR: when two sessions end on the SAME board near-simultaneously, the merge
unions reasons / inprogress / batched / sessions, but `activity`, `written_at`,
`instructions` are taken from the LAST writer only. So the first session's
`activity` summary block is dropped.

WHY ACCEPTED: the actionable payload — `reasons` (the specific gaps) — IS merged,
and the `sessions` list records both participants. `activity` is a coarse counts
summary (edits/ship_signals/etc) that the next SessionStart only uses as soft
context, not to decide which cards to create. Losing one session's counts does
not lose the recon signal. Fixing it (per-session activity map) would bloat the
schema for negligible value.

ALTERNATIVE IF WE REVISIT: store activity under a per-session key, e.g.
out["session_activity"][sid] = act, instead of a single top-level block.

REVISIT IF: a real case shows the dropped activity caused a missed reconciliation,
OR we start surfacing per-session activity in the UI. Until then: INTENTIONAL —
do not "fix" by reintroducing a clobber. Ref commit 0406d2b.

cards.title: Multi-session concurrency hardening (pre-publish stress test + fix races)
cards.origin: test multiple Claudes on same/different boards via permutations; fix what breaks before publishing multi-board
cards.writeup:
Multi-session concurrency hardening — proved the multi-board system under TRUE parallel load and fixed two pre-existing races before publish.

WHAT WE TESTED (new e2e 'concurrency' group, real subprocesses — flock serializes across processes not threads, so every test spawns subprocesses; ThreadPoolExecutor blocks the parent on each concurrently; BOARD_NO_SERVER=1 forces direct-write flock contention; live board untouched):
- same board: 12 parallel card.py add → no lost update, unique titles, rev +12 (board.json CAS/flock from #608/#609 holds).
- different boards: 8 sessions each on own board → no cross-pollution; every session routes to its own board via get_active(sid) — the #611 last-active flock survives concurrent set_active.
- mixed: 3/2/1 across 3 boards → exact per-board counts + per-session routing.
- port assign: 16 (and 50x3 spot-check) distinct new boards first-touched in parallel → distinct ports.
- recon_pending: two sessions ending on same board → both reasons preserved.

RACES FOUND + FIXED (pre-existing defects, surfaced by the harness):
1) port_registry.assign()/set_port() TOCTOU (unlocked read->scan->write): two sessions first-touching DIFFERENT new boards concurrently both read the map empty and picked the SAME lowest-free port -> COLLISION (proven: 14 distinct of 16 without the lock). FIX: generalized the #611 _active_lock into _file_lock(lock_path); assign()/set_port() now wrap the RMW under the assignments lock and re-read inside it. Best-effort/never-raise (degrades to old behaviour on lock timeout).
2) recon_pending.json clobber (bare write_text, no lock): two Stop hooks on the same board overwrote each other's note -> one session's reasons LOST (proven RED without merge). FIX: extracted _write_recon_pending() in _hook_stop_recon.py — locked (per-board .recon_pending.lock) + MERGE (union reasons/inprogress/batched deduped, accumulate a sessions[] list) + atomic tmp+replace. Consumer (hook_session_start counts reasons) unchanged.

VERIFY: concurrency group 18/18; full suite 28 -> 46 passing; live board untouched (452 before/after); both guards proven non-tautological by temporarily reverting each fix -> the matching test goes RED, restore -> green; 50-board distinct-port spot-check 50/50 x3.

Files: skills/e2e/e2e_workboard.py (new concurrency group), scripts/port_registry.py (_file_lock + locked assign/set_port), scripts/_hook_stop_recon.py (_write_recon_pending lock+merge). Revert anchor: #611 committed+pushed first (main 57b469a).

subtasks.text: Concurrency stress harness: new e2e 'concurrency' group — N parallel card.py procs (distinct session ids); same/different/mixed boards + port-assign + recon matrix
[bug] Fix port_registry.assign()/set_port TOCTOU race — flock the RMW (generalize #611 _active_lock → _file_lock); two new boards no longer claim same port
[bug] Fix recon_pending.json clobber — lock + merge reasons so concurrent same-board Stop hooks don't lose each other's notes
Verify: concurrency group green + full suite (28+) + live board untouched + 50-board distinct-port spot-check

code-review fixes: (1) session-key asymmetry — find_board read with raw env while _mark_active wrote via _session_id() → routing silently fell to global on automation paths; centralized identity in port_registry.session_id() used on BOTH sides. (2) recon merge guard clobbered a None-session note; changed to != sid.
🔍 code-review (high): 6 found (2 fixed: session-key asymmetry, recon None-clobber)

reviews.findings: 6 found (2 fixed: session-key asymmetry, recon None-clobber); 1 minor + 3 tradeoffs noted

Same behavior: hover shows full title via body-level tooltip (data-tip), click opens the modal. Files: templates/board.html (.card-readmore CSS = accent link + hover underline; renderCardCompact JS). Verified headless Chrome on live board: 459 cards, 12 truncated, 12 'Read more…' rows, 0 mismatches; tooltips len=2000(#631)/797/493. Visual confirmed on #563. node --check passed.

cards.title: Bootstrap declutter sweep: discovered-only cards (all non-Done cols) → Discarded under dated header
cards.origin: all ONLY Discovered Tags... put in Discarded. keep bug|feature|refactor|enhancement. dont sweep from Done. Sweep from ALL ELSE except done. just change Recon.py so its easier. + a dated Header in Discarded so users arent overwhelmed
cards.notes: GOAL: calm a fresh-bootstrap board so a new user isn't overwhelmed (test board was 135 cards, ALL 'discovered', ~75 noise). RULE (deterministic, no LLM): card with tag 'discovered' AND none of {bug,feature,refactor,enhancement} AND column!=done → Discarded. Sweep ALL non-Done cols; NEVER Done. GUARD: key on 'discovered' tag (only bootstrap mints it) + run ONE-TIME at bootstrap end, NOT recurring SessionStart recon (else a user's new untagged card gets swept). HEADER: dated section-header primitive in Discarded ('🧹 First-run sweep · <date> · N items', collapsible/reversible). IMPL: deterministic pass in hourly_reconcile.py gated to bootstrap replay; header/divider card kind is net-new board.html. NOT started per user ('dont do first').
cards.writeup: Shipped 0.9.22 (commit 95b98ca). declutter_sweep() in scripts/hourly_reconcile.py — a fresh bootstrap can mint 100+ low-signal 'discovered' cards; the sweep moves discovered-only cards (no bug|feature|refactor|enhancement tag) from ALL non-Done columns to Discarded under a dated '🧹 First-run sweep · <date> · N items' section-header. Never touches Done or hand-made (untagged) cards. Runs exactly once at bootstrap end-of-replay, gated. Subsequently improved: one-by-one paced glide (0.9.23 / 4eb9fdf), 45ms glide (#654/889b15d), HUD stays through it + combined tally (#655/20c7c29), settle-then-✓ (#656/9ffbd7d), and the Discarded-on-load + header redesign + collapsed-landing fixes (#650/#651/#653). Card was stale in Task on the restored canonical board — moved to Done to reflect reality.

cards.title: if someone ran more than 5 days ago; i.e me, then it should auto run 7 days,, so extrction should include last conversation?
if haiku fails, run inline? (most liekly not neede)
cards.notes: ASSESSMENT (2026-06-16, after #640/#645/#638): GOOD idea but LOW priority — park, don't build yet. Unique value = the ONE case the retry ladder can't fix: a bucket Haiku PERSISTENTLY can't parse (content-driven non-JSON) but Opus/main-session CAN. Halving (tier-1 chunk-split + tier-2 recursive sub-bucket-at-half) only helps SIZE/token-driven failures, not content/environmental ones. Empirically the ladder has NEVER been observed to exhaust on a bucket that genuinely had cards (8-day differential: exhaustions were legit-empty digests; the '12/13' was a transient hang + the now-fixed silent-success bug). Already 4 layers: tier-1 split, tier-2 recursive halving, #627 in-fill recovery, #645 next-session backfill — all catch TRANSIENT failures. #628 adds value ONLY for PERSISTENT content-failures in a LIVE session (CLAUDECODE=1; autonomous bootstrap unsets it, so needs extra plumbing). REVISIT-TRIGGER: build only if #640 partial:true / #645 backfill logs ever show a persistent (recurring across sessions) ladder-exhaustion on a real bucket. Bigger open lever right now = #647 (time-cap for slowness).
cards.writeup:
FUTURE IMPROVEMENT — parked, not currently needed (assessed 2026-06-16 after #640/#642/#645/#638).

IDEA: when the autonomous Haiku extraction exhausts its retry ladder on a bucket, hand that bucket to the MAIN in-session Claude (Opus, full conversation in context) to extract instead — reusing the inline extraction_pending.json path that already exists (hourly_reconcile._emit_extraction_pending / the CLAUDECODE=1 branch). A 5th, strongest backstop.

WHY IT'S NOT NEEDED NOW: four layers already protect every failure we've actually observed —
  1. tier-1 halving (multi-bucket chunk splits in half),
  2. tier-2 recursive halving (single failed bucket re-buckets at half the minutes, down to 1-min/depth-3),
  3. #627 in-fill recovery pass (one more bounded retry each), and
  4. #645 next-session auto-backfill (re-extracts anything still recorded failed).
Empirically the ladder has NEVER been seen to exhaust on a bucket that genuinely held cards: the 8-day differential debug showed exhaustions were legit-empty digests, and the original '12/13' was a transient subprocess hang + the now-fixed silent-success bug (#640), not a real ladder failure.

THE NARROW NICHE IT UNIQUELY FILLS: halving only helps SIZE/token-driven failures (big digest -> garbled JSON -> smaller parses clean). It does NOTHING for CONTENT-driven failures (text that reliably makes Haiku emit prose not JSON at any size) or ENVIRONMENTAL ones (subprocess can't spawn). #628 is the only fix for a PERSISTENT content-driven failure — and only while a live session exists (CLAUDECODE=1; the detached autonomous bootstrap unsets it, so it would need extra plumbing to fire on the fill path).

BUILD SKETCH (if revisited): in _extract_haiku / run(), when a bucket is still failing after the #627 recovery pass, instead of (or in addition to) recording it in failed_buckets, write it to extraction_pending.json so the main session actions it next turn. Mostly wiring — the inline mechanism already exists.

REVISIT-TRIGGER: build only if #640's partial:true stamp or #645's backfill logs ever show a PERSISTENT (recurring across sessions) ladder-exhaustion on a real, non-empty bucket. Until then this stays an idea. Bigger open lever for the same area = #647 (cap final-bucket extraction time).
cards.origin:
❯ #647 ... and im just wondering: #628 ... do u think its a good idea? so far   
  has it ever failed? even if u havled bucket and timing etc. and so on and     
  forth (for halving?)                                                          
  
⏺ Let me read #628 to answer precisely:
  
⏺ Bash(cd /Users/malco/Desktop/WorkBoard
      python3 - <<'PY'…)
  ⎿  TITLE: if haiku fails, run inline?
     COLUMN: col-akbbnyy
     TAGS: []
     … +3 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ #628 is "if haiku fails, run inline?" — i.e., when the autonomous Haiku
  extraction exhausts its retries on a bucket, hand that bucket to the main 
  in-session Claude (Opus, full context) to extract instead, via the
  extraction_pending.json inline path that already exists. Here's my honest read
  on both your questions.
  
  Has the halving/retry ladder ever actually failed?

  Today there are four layers before a bucket is truly lost:
  1. Tier-1 halving — a multi-bucket chunk that fails splits in half (smaller
  digests parse cleaner).
  2. Tier-2 recursive halving — a single failed bucket re-buckets at half the 
  minutes, recursing down to 1-min / depth 3.
  3. #627 in-fill recovery pass — one more bounded retry of each hard-failed
  bucket.
  4. #645 next-session auto-backfill — re-extracts anything still recorded as
  failed.
  
  Evidence we have: the #627 differential debug ran the extractor over an 8-day
  window at two code states. The ladder did exhaust on a handful of buckets —
  but those were mostly legit-empty (digest deduped to <200 chars → [], not a
  real failure). The original "12/13" incident did not reproduce as a hard
  ladder-exhaustion; it was a transient Haiku-subprocess hang plus the
  silent-success bug (now fixed by #640). So: we have no confirmed case of the
  full ladder exhausting on a bucket that genuinely had cards. Absence of a
  confirmed case isn't proof it can't happen, but empirically the halving has
  been sufficient.

  The key insight on why: halving only helps when failure is size/token-driven
  (big digest → truncated/garbled JSON → smaller chunk parses). It does nothing
  for:
  - content-driven failures (some text reliably makes Haiku emit prose, not
  JSON, at any size), or
  - environmental failures (subprocess can't spawn, model unavailable).
  
  In those cases every sub-bucket fails identically — halving just burns
  retries.

  Is #628 a good idea?
  
  Yes, but low priority — it's a narrow, real niche, not a general win. Its
  unique value is exactly the case halving can't fix: a bucket Haiku
  persistently can't parse but Opus can (content-driven failure, in a live
  session). And the mechanism already exists, so it's mostly wiring, not new
  architecture.

  Why I'd not build it now:
  - The failure it targets is rare and mostly transient — already caught by
  layers 3–4.
  - "Run inline" only works when CLAUDECODE=1 (a live session); the autonomous
  background bootstrap deliberately unsets it, so it wouldn't fire on the
  detached fill path without extra plumbing.
  - It'd be a 5th layer with diminishing returns. The bigger open lever is #647
  (time-cap for slowness), which is a more common pain than reliability now.
  
⏺ Bash(cd /Users/malco/Desktop/WorkBoard
      python3 scripts/card.py update 628 --note "ASSESSMENT (2026-06-16, after
      #640/#645/#638): GOOD idea but LOW priority — park, d…)
  ⎿  ~ #628 notes (rev 873)

⏺ Recorded on #628 with a revisit-trigger so the rationale survives.

  Bottom line: the halving ladder has held up — no observed real-card loss from
  exhaustion — because the failures we've actually seen were transient or
  legit-empty, and those are already covered. #628 is worth keeping for the one
  gap halving structurally can't close (persistent content-driven non-JSON that
  only a stronger model fixes), but there's no evidence yet that gap has ever
  been hit, so it stays parked behind #647.

cards.title: Bootstrap drops most-recent session's cards — NOT the 2d window (downstream extraction)
cards.origin: 0.9.21 reinstall test: bootstrap mined only 2 days, stopped at #581, never captured the latest session's cards (#582-625) and appeared stuck at 12/13.
cards.notes:
DIAGNOSIS (root-cause trace, read-only): (1) WHY 2 DAYS — serve.py --discover-days default=2 (serve.py:803). Chain: hook_session_start.sh → bootstrap_project.sh (passes no days arg, #554 → falls to default) → serve.py(2) → _start_bootstrap_stream → serve_bootstrap → discover2 --days 2 --tier-fly. Deliberate design, documented 'RECOMMENDED: recent/relevant; a larger window surfaces stale cards + worse first board; don't bump casually.' Never overridden on the first-run path. (2) KEY FINDING — the 2d window is NOT why #582-625 were dropped. harvest_jsonl (discover2_sources.py) filters ONLY on a lower bound ('if since and ts < since: continue'), no upper cutoff, and walks ALL ~/.claude/projects/*/*.jsonl incl. the active session. #582-625 were created today = well inside 2 days = ARE harvested. So the miss is at the RECENT end (cutoff exactly at #581), which a lower-bound window cannot cause. => bumping --discover-days will NOT fix this. The real defect is downstream: tier-fly/Haiku extraction or card-numbering stopped short of the newest session's events. NEXT (if pursued): trace serve_bootstrap tier-fly + the Haiku card emitter to see why the most-recent session's events didn't become cards (candidates: tier-1 'last 1d' anchor vs the in-flight session; Haiku timeout/cap; extraction_snapshot staleness).
SCREENSHOT EVIDENCE (9:17PM): HUD = 'BOARD SYNC speeding up ▸▸ backfill / SPEEDING UP 12/13 / 51 card(s) emitted so far / 92%'. So it bugged out at bucket 12 of 13 (92%) in the tier-2 'speedup' phase. Telemetry confirms: card-add emits ran steadily then STOPPED at 13:02:09; .replay_state.json shows completed_card_replay=1 + completed_at 13:02:28 — i.e. it marked the replay COMPLETE despite never finishing bucket 13. Net: a partial fill was treated as final, board capped at #581, no error surfaced to the user. CODE MAP: hourly_extractor._fill_board tier_fly path (L853-908): tier-1 'replay' (recent day, normal pace) runs FIRST, tier-2 'speedup' (older day, 5x pace) SECOND; reconcile+_mark_replay_complete in a try/finally (L898-907) that flips the gate to 1 even if the sweep raises. Card numbers are PRESERVED from transcript mentions (that's why the board was #65-581, not fresh #1-429), so #582-625 are absent because the bucket/chunk carrying them never emitted. OPEN (needs reproduction w/ captured stderr): exact failing bucket-13 cause — candidates: a haiku subprocess hang/timeout on the last chunk, an empty/short final bucket edge case, or the finally-block masking the real exception. NEXT STEP: reproduce in isolation (throwaway board, same --days 2 --tier-fly) capturing serve_bootstrap stderr to get the traceback; do NOT just bump --discover-days (window is not the cause).
DIFFERENTIAL DEBUG (6/16, isolated throwaway boards, live board guard held — 446→447 delta was user's own #628, not leakage). Ran the extractor on the SAME 8-day window at TWO code states: JUNE 9 baseline b3895fa (pre-overhaul) vs HEAD 43a37b2 (post-overhaul). RESULT: functionally IDENTICAL — June9: 162 cards / 70 buckets / 10 non-JSON / 1 zero-drop / exit 0 '✓ complete'; HEAD: 164 / 70 / 11 / 4 / exit 0. Same buckets fail in both (e.g. 2026-06-09 08:30, 23:30). The 162↔164 / 1↔4 deltas are LLM nondeterminism. CONCLUSION: NOT a June-10 regression. git diff 138ab44..HEAD over extraction = hourly_extractor.py UNCHANGED (per-bucket swallow L705-728 cards=[] on non-JSON/exception + _mark_replay_complete byte-identical), digest_compact.py UNCHANGED; only hourly_common.py (prompt: MORE conservative — skip assistant prose/carry-forwards, tighter notes = intentionally fewer cards), hourly_emit.py (+_maybe_review, try/except timeout8), serve_bootstrap.py (review backfill, try/except). None add a crash path. ROOT DEFECT (pre-existing, both versions): SILENT SUCCESS ON PARTIAL FAILURE — a bucket that fails Haiku extraction (non-JSON / exhausted recursive retries) yields [] and the run still prints '✓ emitted N', exits 0, and marks replay complete; nothing surfaces the gap. Some 'zero-drops' are LEGIT-empty (digest deduped to <200c → []). Original 6/10 '12/13 crash' = most likely a TRANSIENT haiku-subprocess hang on the final tier-2 bucket landing on this swallow; did NOT reproduce as a hard crash (8-day single-pass completed clean in BOTH versions). FIX DIRECTION (not yet implemented, per user 'debug first'): surface/halt on bucket-extraction failure + don't mark replay complete when buckets dropped; user idea #628 'if haiku fails, run inline?' = inline-retry fallback for a failed bucket.
SESSION 6/16 — root-cause CONFIRMED + 'stuck 12/13' explained. (A) The '12/13 stuck' is a UI/SSE RACE, not a hang: the HUD 'X/Y' is driven by best-effort fire-and-forget progress (hourly_emit._emit_progress: subprocess card.py progress, timeout 4s, except:pass → card_commands.cmd_progress: POST /progress, urlopen timeout 2s, except:pass; comment says 'cosmetic; HUD won't advance if relay unreachable'). Under tier-2 write contention the final 13/13 SSE event is dropped → HUD freezes on last frame. Proof the WORK finished: .replay_state completed_card_replay=1 (set by _mark_replay_complete which runs only AFTER the tier-2 _run_window returns). So Haiku ran, replay completed; the freeze is display-only. => '12/13' is a RED HERRING, not the defect. (B) Real defect = SILENT SUCCESS ON PARTIAL FAILURE (pre-existing): a bucket failing Haiku extraction (non-JSON/exhausted recursive retries) yields [] and the run still prints '✓ emitted N', exits 0, marks replay complete — the bucket carrying #582-625 emitted 0 cards, dropped silently. (C) DIFFERENTIAL: NOT a June-10 overhaul regression — June9 b3895fa == HEAD on the per-bucket swallow loop (byte-identical); 8-day repro both exit 0 'complete' w/ identical drop pattern. June-10 diff touched only prompt (more conservative) + try/except review-stamps. (D) Haiku variance is HUGE: J9 tier-fly+recon x5 → task 0-21, backlog 14-40, recon-moves 15-36; only 'done' stable (~113). So single-run J9-vs-HEAD column deltas are noise. FIX DIRECTION (unbuilt, user='debug first'): surface/halt on bucket-extraction failure + don't mark replay complete when buckets dropped; user idea (#628 note): if haiku fails on a bucket, retry inline.
cards.writeup: Root defect (silent success on partial bucket-extraction failure) fixed last session via #640 (dc68449): ChunkExtractionError distinguishes failure from empty, retry ladder re-raises when exhausted, failed_buckets recorded + bounded recovery, replay-state stamped partial:true with degraded HUD. Gate-write resilience added in #642 (c5321cf). The '12/13 stuck' was confirmed a UI/SSE display race, not a hang. Verified: dev/test_627_silent_drop.py (17 checks), /e2e 48/0 + recon 8/0, live board untouched. Follow-up #645 auto-backfills recorded failed_buckets on SessionStart.

cards.title: Port-registry last-active pointer races across sessions (cosmetic)
cards.origin: Deferred residual of #609 (#5). ~/.board-steward last-active is a single global pointer; two concurrent sessions both call port_registry.set_active so it ends up pointing at whichever wrote last -> SessionStart hook may open the WRONG board for the human. Cosmetic (no data loss). Optional fix: make set_active/get_active session-aware keyed by CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID, or keep a small MRU list and resolve by the live session. Files: scripts/port_registry.py (ACTIVE_PATH ~line 42), scripts/card.py _mark_active (~line 405), card_state.find_board fallback. Low priority.
cards.writeup:
Session-aware last-active pointer — kills the cross-session clobber that opened the WRONG board under concurrent multi-board sessions (hardening for the multi-board publish).

ROOT CAUSE: ~/.board-steward/last-active was a single global line; two concurrent sessions on different boards both called port_registry.set_active → it pointed at whoever wrote last → SessionStart hook + card.py find_board fallback resolved the wrong board.

FIX (session-aware, back-compat):
- port_registry.py: last-active is now JSON {global, sessions[sid]}. set_active(board, session_id=None) updates global (most-recent, fresh-session fallback) AND the caller's session slot, under a best-effort inlined flock (mirrors _boardio.board_lock; never raises/blocks — degrades to today on timeout). get_active(session_id=None) is tiered self-heal: session's own board if live → else global if live → else None. _read_active reads the OLD single-line format transparently (no migration step). _prune_sessions caps the map at 32 newest + drops dead-on-disk entries (size cap, never an age TTL, so a long-running session keeps its slot).
- card.py _mark_active: passes card_commands._session_id() (explicit import — the underscore name is not exported by 'import *').
- card_state.py find_board: passes CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID (read from env directly to stay import-light) → a session resolves ITS board.

cards.title: Warn before Cmd+Z undo when board changed in another session
cards.origin: Deferred residual of #609 (#3). Browser undo/redo restores a full snapshot and POSTs with NO base-rev header (browser stays last-writer-wins by design — #609 scope), so a Cmd+Z can silently overwrite another session's concurrent edits. LIGHT-TOUCH FIX (chosen over full browser CAS, which would let a user's drag get rejected and hurt UX): in templates/board.html undo()/redo() handlers (~line 3286-3294 and the redo path), BEFORE applying the snapshot, fetch /rev (cheap) and if it != lastRev/state.rev (board moved elsewhere), show a confirm: 'Board changed in another session — undo may overwrite their work. Proceed?' Default = cancel. No server change needed. Test: open two sessions, move a card in S2, then Cmd+Z in S1's board window -> should prompt.
cards.writeup:
Removed the false-firing undo/redo "Board changed in another session — may overwrite their work. Proceed?" popup (auto-accept), per user ("auto = accept to prevent UI").

ROOT CAUSE
_confirmRestoreIfBoardMoved (templates/board.html) probed /rev and fired window.confirm on ANY rev delta (j.rev !== lastRev). The agent's own card.py writes bump rev constantly, so from the browser it looked like "another session changed the board" on nearly every Cmd/Ctrl+Z — a false positive, made more visible when testing multiple ?sid sessions. The guard was belt-and-suspenders: the board is last-writer-wins by design (#609) and undo is reversible (pushes prior state onto the redo stack).

CHANGE (templates/board.html, single file)
- Deleted async function _confirmRestoreIfBoardMoved (the /rev probe + confirm), replaced with a one-line #610 comment explaining the removal.
- Removed its two guarded calls in undo() (~3440) and redo() (~3479). All other undo/redo logic untouched (blur editor, snapshot push/pop, _tagRestoreMoves, scheduleSave, render, toast).
- Dropped a needless per-undo /rev fetch as a bonus.

HISTORY (user asked "was this done 2-3 days ago?")
- No — the guard landed yesterday 2026-06-16 (commit 8bffbb2 "#610: warn before Cmd+Z undo..."). Sibling same-day multi-session work: #611 (57b469a session-aware last-active), #633 (0406d2b concurrency hardening), #643 (ce03382 rev-CAS direct writes). This change does NOT touch that CAS/last-active machinery — #610 was an independent undo-vs-LWW guard layered on top.

VERIFICATION (/code-review run before commit, per user — result: clean, 0 findings)
- grep "Board changed in another session" → only in the explanatory comment now (live confirm gone).
- grep "_confirmRestoreIfBoardMoved" → 0 (function + both callers removed, no lingering refs repo-wide).
- REV_URL still used by the 3s poll (line 3541) — not orphaned.
- undo()/redo() called fire-and-forget from keydown (undo();), so removing the await/return gate breaks no caller; they stay async (valid).
- Served live from templates/board.html (no rebuild) — reload :7891 picks it up.

NOT committed/pushed — gating publish on user OK (version bump + marketplace sync to follow when approved).

subtasks.text: Undo/redo 'Board changed in another session' window.confirm false-fires on EVERY undo (any rev delta — incl. this agent's own card.py writes bump rev), so it pops up constantly with multiple SIDs/sessions. User wants it auto-accepted to prevent the UI. Relax _confirmRestoreIfBoardMoved (templates/board.html:3424) so it never blocks. Plan + /code-review before commit per user.

cards.title: Multi-session write-race hardening (rev-as-CAS, no unlocked fallback, cross-session undo)
cards.origin: #602 audit found data-integrity bugs under N concurrent sessions, scarier than the pulse (which #608 fixed): (1) _boardio.py:71-72 board_lock TIMES OUT after 5s then writes UNLOCKED -> two card.py both read rev N, both write N+1 = lost card update; (2) card_state.py:190 rev is a counter not a CAS, no conflict check; (3) board.html undo stacks are browser-local -> cross-session Cmd+Z clobbers another session's saved work; (4) recon stale-snapshot can move the wrong card; (5) port_registry last-active single pointer opens wrong board. CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID (added in #608) is the enabling primitive. Fix: rev compare-and-swap on POST, drop the unlocked fallback, session-tag writes.
cards.notes: CAS CORE SHIPPED @f6b232a (branch feat/per-session-pulse): rev-as-CAS on agent writes + 12x jittered retry + no-unlocked-fallback. /code-review done (1 real finding fixed: cache-drift disk re-check; 1 cosmetic accepted). Verified: stale=409, 12x parallel all land, drift heals, lock-held fails. REMAINING (reassess w/ user): #3 cross-session undo (needs browser CAS — conflicts w/ chosen last-writer-wins), #4 recon stale-reason (largely auto-mitigated by CAS+recon_lock), #5 port-registry pointer (cosmetic).
cards.writeup: CAS CORE SHIPPED @f6b232a (branch feat/per-session-pulse). Fixed the dangerous bug: silent agent-vs-agent lost card writes. serve.py enforces rev compare-and-swap on agent POSTs (X-Board-Base-Rev) + on cache/disk mismatch re-checks on-disk rev before rejecting; card_state.py BoardConflict + sends base rev + no-unlocked-fallback; card.py main() 12x jittered-backoff retry on conflict + direct path refuses unlocked write. /code-review: multi-save double-apply REFUTED (saves are branch-exclusive); cache-drift 409-storm finding FIXED; cosmetic retry-reprint accepted. Verified: stale=409, 12 parallel adds all land (none lost/dup), drift heals, lock-held fails, 6 dev tests pass. DEFERRED to follow-ups: #3 cross-session undo (#611), #5 port-registry (#612). #4 recon stale-reason needs NO work — auto-mitigated by CAS+retry on recon's card.py subprocess moves + existing recon_lock.

cards.title: Per-session active-work pulse: max ONE pulse per session, N sessions = N pulsing cards
cards.origin: #602 — current activeWorkId is a single scalar in board.json so concurrent sessions clobber (last-writer-wins) and only one card can ever pulse. Generalize to per-session: activeWork map {sessionId: cardId}, pulse every value, steal is per-session (agent move from session S replaces only S's entry). card.py reads CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID env to tag its claim. Needs stale-session prune (SessionStart/Stop hook) so dead sessions don't leave ghost pulses. Keep #587 agent-only-claim + #589 undo-no-steal semantics, just per-session. Scope: PULSE ONLY (write-race hardening tracked separately).
cards.writeup: Per-session active-work pulse shipped on branch feat/per-session-pulse (commit a6471a9). activeWorkId scalar -> activeWork map {sessionId:{cardId,ts}}: N sessions = N pulsing cards, max one per session. Agent claims via CLAUDE_CODE_SESSION_ID; browser never claims (agent-only #587/#589 kept), adopts authoritative map from rev-bumped broadcast. Self-heal on leave-IP + 12h TTL backstop for ghost pulses; per-session one-in-flight guard; legacy scalar migrates to _legacy. Top-pin uses ?sid (board_autoopen passes it) with most-recent fallback. Verified: 2 sessions->2 entries, per-session guard, self-heal, TTL, migration; all 6 dev tests pass. PENDING: merge to main + board server reinstall/restart to take effect on the live board (currently serves old template). Out-of-scope follow-up noted: multi-session write-race hardening (rev-as-CAS, no unlocked fallback, cross-session undo).

cards.title: Empirical A/B: bootstrap card-volume before vs after #599 (current7 vs reconly7)
cards.origin: run the one BEFORE these changes for the bootstrap carding for code review — make sure empirically nothing much changed, like reconly5/6. call it reconly7 (add code review in LIVE and bootstrap)
cards.notes: current7 = baseline @e46c1c8 (pre-#599, clean worktree); reconly7 = HEAD @845e776 (with #599 code-review backfill in LIVE+bootstrap). 3 runs x 3 days each, same real history window. Confirm #599 did not shift extraction volume/columns; reviewed-stamp should appear only in reconly7.
cards.writeup:
Empirical A/B, 3x3-day Haiku bootstrap, same real history window. current7 (@e46c1c8, BEFORE #599) vs reconly7/longconv3 (@HEAD, WITH #599).

KEY FINDING (resolves 'is the extra backlog/task/ip caused by the code-review change?'): NO. Proven two ways: (1) git diff 6ab1b9e..e46c1c8 over the entire extractor+reconciler (hourly_*, discover2_*) is EMPTY → current7's engine is byte-identical to reconly6, i.e. current7 IS the reconly6-equivalent baseline. (2) The LIVE code-review change (#598) added card.py review/queries/Stop-hook only — does not touch bootstrap; #599 is the SOLE commit between current7 and reconly7.

Medians: reconly6 (OLD window) total107 task7 ip6 back7; current7 (TODAY window, SAME code, NO #599) total126 task10 ip11 back9; reconly7 (current7+#599) total117 task12 ip8 back6. The 107→126 inflation appears in current7 which has NO #599 → it is driven by today being a huge day (more raw work to extract), NOT the feature. current7→reconly7: backlog 9→6, ip 11→8 (DOWN), so #599 does not add backlog. The reviewed stamp (0→7-8/run) only tags Done cards, never moves cards to task/backlog. No bug-out either state. Data: dev/bench_results/results.jsonl labels current7/longconv3/reconly5/reconly6.

cards.origin: do multiple 3x3 days simulation and bootstrap to see if bootstrap bugs out cos today we had a LONG conversation (ref reconly5/reconly6 card-move counts)
cards.notes: 3 runs x 3-day Haiku bootstrap via dev/bench_recon.sh over real history. 3-day window now includes today's huge session → tests extractor robustness (timeouts/stranded cards/crashes) AND exercises #599 review-backfill extraction changes on real data. Compare card volume + recon→done/backlog moves vs reconly5/6 baseline.
cards.writeup: 3x3-day Haiku bootstrap over real history incl. today's huge session. NO BUG-OUT: all 3 runs completed (117/125/110 cards), no traceback/crash/exit-code/zero-run. Only log hits = normal self-healing (↻ retry: splitting failed chunk on Haiku timeout — a feature). IP piles small (13/8/5). #599 review-backfill confirmed firing in real bootstrap: 7-8 cards/run got the reviewed stamp via the LLM path. Higher volume (~110-125 vs reconly5/6 ~54) is just today being a big day. recon→done 0/10/10, recon→backlog 0/1/6. Results in dev/bench_results/results.jsonl label=longconv3.

cards.title: i rmb u did the puslate today, where i explained to you how i wanted it, i forgot if THERE MULTIPLE sessions working on differnet cards, then it should have MULTIPLE pulsating. so i guess the workaround would still be. SAME protocol, but THE MAX number of pulsating IS ONE PER SESSION. does that make sense? so first, see todays convo history about pulsating. so what i have in mind is eg. session 1  (Card A) Pulsates. then another card moves and Card A is STILL STICKY. (as per CURRENT CODE). HOWEVER, if there IS ANOTHER SESSION, THEN the SAME protocol, BUT NOW THERE CAN BE TWO PULSATING CARDS. (CAn you find the convo where i explained to you on Card A and Card B?) also i found out, if we have multiple session, what else breaks? do a /code-review and explore agent to identify if it breaks?
cards.writeup:
Done — the requested feature shipped under #608/#609; this card was just never moved.

WHAT WAS ASKED
- Pulsating should be MAX ONE PER SESSION, but multiple concurrent sessions → multiple pulsating cards (Card A pulses in session 1 and stays sticky; if session 2 claims Card B, both pulse).
- Plus: with multiple sessions, "what else breaks?" — run /code-review + explore agent.

WHERE IT LANDED
- #608 (commit a6471a9, "per-session active-work pulse — N sessions = N pulsing cards") implements exactly this. Live in templates/board.html: state.activeWork = {sid: {cardId, ts}} is a per-session pulse map; each session pulses its own most-recently-claimed card, other sessions' cards keep pulsing where they sit; the auto-opened URL carries ?sid so the board knows which of the N pulses is "mine." Sticky behavior preserved (new IP arrivals don't steal the glow), undo restores every session's pulse.
- #609 (commit f6b232a) fixed the related multi-session hazard: rev-as-CAS on agent writes to stop silent lost card updates (concurrent sessions clobbering each other).
- Released in 0.9.21 (8c011a4); memory note 260610 records MERGED + DEPLOYED + VERIFIED (12 parallel adds none lost, stale→409).
- The "what else breaks with multiple sessions" review was done — it produced the accepted concurrency tradeoffs cards #633–#637 (recon activity-merge, get_active global fallback, prune 32-cap, file-lock timeout), each with a revisit trigger.

VERIFICATION
- Confirmed the #608 per-session pulse code is present in the current templates/board.html (not reverted): lines ~6162–6880 reference the per-session pulse map, ?sid resolution, and server-authoritative adoption.
